memoryArtifacts.headline: Armorial Achievement of Holland of Denton
memoryArtifacts.description: Arms.— Quarterly: 1, 4. Azure [semée of fleurs-de-lis] a lion rampant guardant, between six fleurs-de-lis, argent, over all a bend gules [Holland]; 2, 3. Sable [argent], a cross lozengy argent [sable] …

zh.Prescot, Lancashire, England: 英國藍卡爾普雷斯可特
zh.lifeSummary: 當Richard Holland of Denton IV於1443年出生在英國藍卡爾丹頓時，他的父親Sir Richard de Holland I23歲，母親Elizabeth de Southworth21歲。他於1467年與Isabella Harrington在英國藍卡爾普雷斯可特結婚。他們至少是5個兒子和2個女兒的父母。他去世於1501年3月5日在他的家鄉享年58歲，埋葬在英國藍卡爾丹頓。

zh-hans.Prescot, Lancashire, England: 英国藍卡爾普雷斯可特
zh-hans.lifeSummary: 当Richard Holland of Denton IV于1443年出生在英国藍卡爾丹頓时，他的父亲Sir Richard de Holland I23岁，母亲Elizabeth de Southworth21岁。他于1467年与Isabella Harrington在英国藍卡爾普雷斯可特结婚。他们至少是5个儿子和2个女儿的父母。他去世于1501年3月5日在他的家乡享年58岁，埋葬在英国藍卡爾丹頓。

nameMeanings.name: Holland
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English, German, Dutch, Danish, Norwegian, Swedish, French, and Jewish (Ashkenazic): habitational name from Holland, a province of the Netherlands.

English: habitational name from Downholland or Upholland (Lancashire), Hulland (Derbyshire), the Parts of Holland, one of the three administrative subdivisions of Lincolnshire, any of the four places called Hoyland (southern Yorkshire), and possibly Great and Little Holland (Essex). The placenames all derive from Old English hōh ‘heel, spur of land’ + land ‘land’.

English: habitational name either from Hoeland (Farm) in Bury (Sussex), or from Holland's Barn in Albourne (Sussex). The placename in Bury has the same etymology as in 1 above, while the placename in Albourne may derive from Old English hol ‘hole, hollow’ + land ‘land’.

variants.name: Hollands
meanings: English: variant of Holland , especially 3, with post-medieval excrescent -s.
Dutch: genitivized form of   Holland  .

variants.name: Howland
meanings: English: variant of   Holland  .
Americanized form of Norwegian   Hovland  .

History: Howland was the name of three Quaker brothers, original settlers in Marshfield, MA. They were from Huntingdonshire, England. The eldest, John Howland (c. 1593–1672) was a passenger on the Mayflower, servant to Gov. John Carver, who died in the first winter at Plymouth Colony.

variants.name: Hollander
meanings: Dutch (also Den Hollander), German (also Holländer), English, Swedish, and Jewish (Ashkenazic): habitational name for someone from Holland (see Holland 1).

variants.name: Holand
meanings: English and Scottish: variant of   Holland  .
Norwegian: habitational name from about twenty farmsteads so named, composed of Old Norse hár ‘high’ + land ‘land, field’, denoting a high-lying farm.

variants.name: Aland
meanings: Scandinavian (Åland): topographic name from the Åland Islands in the Gulf of Bothnia.

English: topographic name from Middle English atte lande (Old French laund) ‘dweller at the glade or pasture’, or sometimes perhaps, ‘dweller at the old cultivated land(s)’ from Old English eald, ald ‘old’ + land ‘land’.

variants.name: Hollon
meanings: English: altered form of   Holland  .

nameMeanings.name: of
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Korean Kyong, Kyong Suk, Young, Chae, Chi Young, Hang, Kil, Rin, Sang Hun, Chang, Chong, Insoo, Kum, Sun Young, Yom, Young Hwan.  Cambodian Kiwan, Oeurn, Phoeun, Pich, Saroeuth, Seung, Sophath, Sophea, Yoeun, Phan, Son.  Indian Shanti, Harendra.
Indian (Uttar Pradesh): Brahmin name, from Sanskrit om, the Hindu sacred and mystic syllable uttered at the beginning of prayers.
Cambodian: written ឱម, corresponding to the exclamation of solemn affirmation, of Sanskrit origin (see 1 above), chanted by Buddhist monks.

variants.name: Oum
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Southeast Asian Heang, Hye, Kam, Kan, Kheng, Khin, Kun, Sang, Sok, Phong, Yom, Yoo, Youn, Cuong, Phal, Samnang, Tin.  Cambodian Sokha, Chheang, Choeun, Moeun, Roeun, Sambath, Samoeun, Savuth, Soeun, Sopheap.
Cambodian: written អ៊ុំ, from a Khmer word denoting an elder sibling of one's parent (i.e. ‘uncle’ or ‘aunt’). Compare   Om   3 and   Um   2.

nameMeanings.name: Denton
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English: habitational name from any of numerous places so called. The vast majority, including those in Cambridgeshire, Cumbria, Dumfries, Durham, Kent, Lancashire, Lincolnshire, Norfolk, Northumberland, Oxfordshire, Sussex, and Yorkshire are named from Old English denu ‘valley’ (see Dean 1) + tūn ‘enclosure, settlement’. An isolated example in Northamptonshire appears in the Domesday Book as Dodintone meaning ‘enclosure, settlement associated with Dodda or Dudda’.

variants.name: Dean
meanings: English: topographic name from Middle English dene ‘valley’ (Old English denu), or a habitational name from any of several places in various parts of England named Dean or Dene from this word.

English: nickname or occupational name for the servant of a dean or nickname for someone thought to resemble a dean. A dean was an ecclesiastical official, the head of a chapter of canons or a church official with jurisdiction over a sub-division of an archdeaconry. Though no doubt some deans had illegitimate children, they were officially celibate, and in the main the surname is probably a nickname in origin, similar to Bishop , Prior , Priest , and Monk . The Middle English word deen, dien, dein, is a borrowing of Old French d(e)ien, doien from Latin decanus (originally a leader of ten men, from decem ‘ten’), and thus is a cognate of Deacon .
English: from the Middle English personal name Deyne (or Dene) a rhyming pet form of Reynald (see Reginald ).

nameMeanings.name: Richard
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: One of the most enduringly successful of the Old French personal names introduced into Britain by the Normans. It is of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from rīc ‘power’ + hard ‘strong, hardy’. It has enjoyed continuous popularity in England from the Conquest to the present day, influenced by the fact that it was borne by three kings of England, in particular Richard I ( 1157–99 ). He was king for only ten years ( 1189–99 ), most of which he spent in warfare abroad, taking part in the Third Crusade and costing the people of England considerable sums in taxes. Nevertheless, he achieved the status of a folk hero, and was never in England long enough to disappoint popular faith in his goodness and justice. He was also Duke of Aquitaine and Normandy and Count of Anjou, fiefs which he held at a time of maximum English expansion in France. His exploits as a leader of the Third Crusade earned him the nickname ‘Coeur de Lion’ or ‘Lionheart’ and a permanent place in popular imagination, in which he was even more firmly enshrined by Sir Walter Scott's novel Ivanhoe ( 1820 ).

variants.name: Rikert
meanings: One of the most enduringly successful of the Old French personal names introduced into Britain by the Normans. It is of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from rīc ‘power’ + hard ‘strong, hardy’. It has enjoyed continuous popularity in England from the Conquest to the present day, influenced by the fact that it was borne by three kings of England, in particular Richard I ( 1157–99 ). He was king for only ten years ( 1189–99 ), most of which he spent in warfare abroad, taking part in the Third Crusade and costing the people of England considerable sums in taxes. Nevertheless, he achieved the status of a folk hero, and was never in England long enough to disappoint popular faith in his goodness and justice. He was also Duke of Aquitaine and Normandy and Count of Anjou, fiefs which he held at a time of maximum English expansion in France. His exploits as a leader of the Third Crusade earned him the nickname ‘Coeur de Lion’ or ‘Lionheart’ and a permanent place in popular imagination, in which he was even more firmly enshrined by Sir Walter Scott's novel Ivanhoe ( 1820 ).

meanings: One of the most enduringly successful of the Old French personal names introduced into Britain by the Normans. It is of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from rīc ‘power’ + hard ‘strong, hardy’. It has enjoyed continuous popularity in England from the Conquest to the present day, influenced by the fact that it was borne by three kings of England, in particular Richard I ( 1157–99 ). He was king for only ten years ( 1189–99 ), most of which he spent in warfare abroad, taking part in the Third Crusade and costing the people of England considerable sums in taxes. Nevertheless, he achieved the status of a folk hero, and was never in England long enough to disappoint popular faith in his goodness and justice. He was also Duke of Aquitaine and Normandy and Count of Anjou, fiefs which he held at a time of maximum English expansion in France. His exploits as a leader of the Third Crusade earned him the nickname ‘Coeur de Lion’ or ‘Lionheart’ and a permanent place in popular imagination, in which he was even more firmly enshrined by Sir Walter Scott's novel Ivanhoe ( 1820 ).

historicEvents.title: Wars of the Roses
historicEvents.description: Wars of the Roses was a series of English civil wars for control of the throne of England between the royal families House of Lancaster and the House of York. The name of the war was derived as the red rose was the symbol of the Lancastrians and the white rose was the symbol of the Yorks.

en.lifeSummary: When Richard Holland of Denton IV was born in 1443, in Denton, Lancashire, England, his father, Sir Richard de Holland I, was 23 and his mother, Elizabeth de Southworth, was 21. He married Isabella Harrington in 1467, in Prescot, Lancashire, England. They were the parents of at least 5 sons and 2 daughters. He died on 5 March 1501, in his hometown, at the age of 58, and was buried in Denton, Lancashire, England.

fr.lifeSummary: Lorsque Richard Holland of Denton IV est né en 1443, à Denton, Lancashire, Angleterre, son père, Sir Richard de Holland I, avait 23 ans et sa mère, Elizabeth de Southworth, avait 21 ans. Il a épousé Isabella Harrington en 1467, à Prescot, Lancashire, Angleterre. Ils ont eu au moins 5 fils et 2 filles. Il est décédé le 5 mars 1501, dans sa ville natale, à l’âge de 58 ans, et a été enterré à Denton, Lancashire, Angleterre.

de.lifeSummary: Als Richard Holland of Denton IV 1443 in Denton, Lancashire, England geboren wurde, war sein Vater Sir Richard de Holland I 23 und seine Mutter Elizabeth de Southworth 21. Er hat 1467 in Prescot, Lancashire, England Isabella Harrington geheiratet. Sie hatten mindestens 5 Söhne und 2 Töchter. Er starb am 5. März 1501 im Alter von 58 Jahren in seinem Geburtsort und wurde in Denton, Lancashire, England begraben.

it.lifeSummary: Quando Richard Holland of Denton IV è nato il nel 1443 nel Denton, Lancashire, England, il suo padre, Sir Richard de Holland I, aveva 23 anni e il suo madre, Elizabeth de Southworth, aveva 21 anni. Lui sposato Isabella Harrington nel 1467, nel Prescot, Lancashire, England. Erano genitori di almeno 5 figli e 2 figlie. Morì il 5 marzo 1501, nel sua cittadina, all'età di 58 anni, e fu sepolto a Denton, Lancashire, England.

ja.Prescot, Lancashire, England: イギリスランカシュアイアプレスコト
ja.Denton, Lancashire, England: イギリスランカシュアイアデントン
ja.lifeSummary: Richard Holland of Denton IVが1443年にイギリスランカシュアイアデントンで生まれた時，父Sir Richard de Holland Iは23歳，母Elizabeth de Southworthは21歳であった。1467年にイギリスランカシュアイアプレスコトでIsabella Harringtonと結婚した。彼らは少なくとも5人の息子と2人の娘の親であった。彼は1501年3月5日に故郷で58歳で死去し，イギリスランカシュアイアデントンにて埋葬された。

ko.lifeSummary: Richard Holland of Denton IV(이)가 1443년 영국 랭카실르 덴턴에서 태어났을 때, 그의 아버지 Sir Richard de Holland I(은)는 23세였고, 그의 어머니 Elizabeth de Southworth(은)는 21세였다. 그는 1467년 영국 랭카실르 프레스컷에서 Isabella Harrington(와)과 결혼했다. 그들은 적어도 다섯 명의 아들과 두 명의 딸을 두었다. 그는 1501년 3월 5일 그의 고향에서 58세의 나이로 사망했으며 영국 랭카실르 덴턴에 묻혔다.

pt.lifeSummary: Quando Richard Holland of Denton IV nasceu em 1443, em Denton, Lancashire, Inglaterra, seu pai, Sir Richard de Holland I, tinha 23 anos e sua mãe, Elizabeth de Southworth, tinha 21 anos. Ele casou-se com Isabella Harrington em 1467, em Prescot, Lancashire, Inglaterra. Eles tiveram pelo menos 5 filhos e 2 filhas. Ele faleceu em 5 de março de 1501, em sua cidade natal, com 58 anos, e foi sepultado em Denton, Lancashire, Inglaterra.

ru.lifeSummary: Когда Richard Holland of Denton IV родился в 1443 г. в местности дентон, ланкашир, Англия, его отцу по имени Sir Richard de Holland I было 23 года, а матери, которую звали Elizabeth de Southworth, 21. Он женился на женщине по имени Isabella Harrington в 1467 г. в местности прескот, ланкашир, Англия. У них было по крайней мере 5 сыновей и 2 дочери. Он умер 5 марта 1501 г. в его родном городе в возрасте 58 лет и был похоронен в местности дентон, ланкашир, Англия.

es.lifeSummary: Cuando Richard Holland of Denton IV nació en 1443, en Denton, Lancashire, Inglaterra, su papá, Sir Richard de Holland I, tenía 23 años y su mamá, Elizabeth de Southworth, tenía 21 años. Se casó con Isabella Harrington en 1467, en Prescot, Lancashire, Inglaterra. Fueron padres de por lo menos 5 hijos y 2 hijas. Murió el 5 de marzo de 1501, en su pueblo, a la edad de 58 años, y fue sepultado en Denton, Lancashire, Inglaterra.
